text: The surgeon makes a transverse incision in the peritoneum from the anterior superior iliac spine towards the medial umbilical cord. The preperitoneal space is entered, and a preperitoneal flap is created. Dissection proceeds medially towards the symphysis pubis and pubic bone, and the hernia sac is reduced from the hernia defect.

The surgeon carefully identifies and dissects the testicular vessels and vas deferens, which are significantly attached to the large hernia sac, ensuring these vital structures are separated without injury.

The video shows the dissection of the vas deferens, which is completely separated from the hernia sac. The vesicular vessels remain attached to the sac, highlighting the need for careful attention to the anatomy to prevent injury to these blood vessels.

The hernia sac is shown completely dissected away from the blood vessels. The vas deferens and testicular vessels are visibly isolated and protected. The dissection progresses inferiorly, superiorly, and laterally to create a pocket for mesh placement.

A three-dimensional Marlex mesh is inserted into the pre-peritoneal space, unfolded, and positioned to cover the hernia defect. The mesh is secured to the abdominal wall using a biome loop. The surgeon closes the peritoneal defect with two OV lock absorbable sutures in a running fashion, ensuring the hernia site is included to reinforce the peritoneum.
